sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 59, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'moves player 4x4 avatar right by 6 cols to next chamber (VERIFIED steps 1,3,4)', 'DOWN': 'only shrinks HUD timer at [8,20] (step 2); untested at other positions', 'LEFT': 'untested', 'UP': 'untested', 'SPACE': 'untested'}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_square_black_center': 'player avatar, 4x4 blue square with 1 black center pixel, 24 pixels total', 'blue_square_blue_center': 'target/goal, 6x6 blue square with 1 blue center pixel, 19 pixels, at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]', 'white_paths': 'maze corridors forming S-shaped path through black walls', 'red_path': 'red colored path on right side of maze, 82 pixels', 'white_square': 'white 5x5 square at [14,20]-[18,24], possibly another target or obstacle'}
- HUD map: {'row_63': 'blue timer strip, shrinks by 1 pixel per action (64→63→...)'}
- Win pattern: Reach the blue target square at bottom-right with the player avatar
Current level:
- World model: Maze with chambers connected by white corridors. Player is 4x4 blue square that jumps between chambers. Chambers in top row at cols 14, 20, 26, 32 (spaced 6 apart). White path forms S-shape: top-left → top-right → down right side → left → down → right to bottom. Player currently at [8,32] after 3 RIGHTs.
- Goal model: Navigate player from start (top-left) through maze chambers to blue target at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]
- Recent findings: ['Player moved: [8,14]→[8,20]→[8,26]→[8,32] with RIGHT', 'DOWN at [8,20] only changed HUD timer, no player movement', 'Player is now at [8,32] near red path area']
- Open questions: ['Does DOWN work at [8,32] (just tested, awaiting result)?', 'What is the full chamber connectivity graph?', 'Can LEFT reverse movement?', 'What does SPACE do?']
- Plan: Test DOWN at [8,32]. If it works, continue following the S-shaped white corridor path: down right side, then left, then down, then right to target. If not, try RIGHT more or LEFT back to find working DOWN position.
- Failed probes: ['DOWN at [8,20] - no player movement, only HUD timer changed (step 2)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 58 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24 and n['children']:
            return n
    return None

p = find_player(current_frame.segmentation)
print(f"Player: {p['boundary'][0] if p else '?'}")

# Go to [20,14] and try RIGHT
action(['LEFT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "moves player to next chamber; behavior varies by position", "DOWN": "works at [8,38]→[14,26], at [14,26]→[20,14], at [20,14]→[20,20], at [8,32]→[14,14]; no effect at [20,20], [20,26], [8,20], [8,26], [8,14]", "LEFT": "moves player to previous chamber; behavior varies by position. LEFT at [20,14]→[20,20] (moves right!), LEFT at [20,20]→[20,14] (moves left)", "UP": "moves player up; [20,26]→[14,26]; no effect at [20,20]", "SPACE": "resets player to start [8,14]"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_square_black_center": "player avatar (4x4)", "blue_square_blue_center": "target/goal"}, "hud_map": {"row_63": "blue timer strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at [20,20]. Chamber graph: [8,14]-[8,38], [8,38]→[14,26], [8,32]→[14,14], [14,14]→[20,14], [14,26]→[20,26] or [20,14], [20,14]↔[20,20]↔[20,26]. LEFT at [20,14]→[20,20] (moves right!).", "goal_model": "Navigate to blue target at bottom right", "recent_findings": ["LEFT at [20,14]→[20,20] (moves right!)"], "open_questions": ["Where does RIGHT from [20,14] go?"], "current_plan": "Try RIGHT from [20,14] to find new chamber", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["DOWN at [20,26]", "DOWN at [20,20] (twice)", "UP at [20,20]", "DOWN at [8,26]", "DOWN at [8,20]", "RIGHT at [20,26]", "DOWN at [8,14]"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT from [20,14] went to [20,20]. So LEFT from [20,14] → [20,20], which means LEFT moves the player right by 6 cols in this case. That's weird - it seems like LEFT and RIGHT might be swapped, or the maze has some kind of wrapping behavior.
Wait, let me reconsider. I previously thought LEFT from [20,20] → [20,14], which would mean LEFT moves left by 6 cols. But now LEFT from [20,14] → [20,20], which would mean LEFT moves right by 6 cols. This is contradictory.
Let me re-examine. Maybe I was wrong about the previous LEFT behavior. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, looking at the world model I've been maintaining, I said LEFT moves player left by 6 cols, [20,20]→[20,14]. But now LEFT from [20,14] → [20,20], which is right by 6 cols.
This is confusing. Let me think about what's happening. Maybe LEFT doesn't always move left - maybe it moves to the next chamber in some direction based on the maze layout?
Or maybe I was wrong about the previous LEFT behavior. Let me check the history.

- [20,14] - left
- [20,20] - middle
- [20,26] - right
But LEFT from [20,14] → [20,20] means LEFT moves right, which is counterintuitive. Unless the maze has some kind of wrapping or the chambers are connected differently than I thought.
Wait, maybe I'm misunderstanding the chamber positions. Let me look at the image again. The player is at [20,20] now, which is the middle chamber. The left chamber would be at [20,14] and the right chamber at [20,26].
But LEFT from [20,14] → [20,20] means LEFT moves right. This is very strange.
Actually, I think I might have made an error in my previous analysis. Let me re-examine the history.
Looking at the image, the player is at [20,20] now. The previous position was [20,14]. So LEFT from [20,14] → [20,20]. This means LEFT moves the player right by 6 cols, which is the opposite of what I expected.
Hmm, but this contradicts my earlier observation that LEFT from [20,20] → [20,14]. Let me check if that was correct.

But this contradicts my earlier observation that LEFT from [20,20] → [20,14]. Unless LEFT has different behavior depending on the position.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe LEFT doesn't always move left - maybe it moves to the next chamber in the direction of the white path? Or maybe it moves to the chamber that is connected via the white corridor?
Looking at the image, the white path seems to form a specific shape. Let me trace it:
- From the top left, the white path goes right to the top right
- Then down the right side
- Then left to the middle left
- Then down to the bottom left
- Then right to the bottom right (target)
So the white path forms a spiral or S-shape. Maybe LEFT and RIGHT move the player along this path, rather than literally left and right?
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the entire maze structure. Let me look at the image more carefully.

- There's a blue target at the bottom right
The white path seems to form a specific shape. Let me trace it:
- From the top left, the white path goes right to the top right
- Then down the right side
- Then left to the middle left
- Then down to the bottom left
- Then right to the bottom right (target)
So the white path forms a spiral or S-shape. Maybe LEFT and RIGHT move the player along this path?
Actually, I think the key insight is that the maze has a specific connectivity pattern, and LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN move the player to connected chambers, not necessarily in the literal direction.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24 and n['children']:
            return n
    return None

p = find_player(current_frame.segmentation)
print(f"Player: {p['boundary'][0] if p else '?'}")

# Go to [20,14] and try RIGHT
action(['LEFT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "moves player to next chamber; behavior varies by position", "DOWN": "works at [8,38]→[14,26], at [14,26]→[20,14], at [20,14]→[20,20], at [8,32]→[14,14]; no effect at [20,20], [20,26], [8,20], [8,26], [8,14]", "LEFT": "moves player to previous chamber; behavior varies by position. LEFT at [20,14]→[20,20] (moves right!), LEFT at [20,20]→[20,14] (moves left)", "UP": "moves player up; [20,26]→[14,26]; no effect at [20,20]", "SPACE": "resets player to start [8,14]"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_square_black_center": "player avatar (4x4)", "blue_square_blue_center": "target/goal"}, "hud_map": {"row_63": "blue timer strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at [20,20]. Chamber graph: [8,14]-[8,38], [8,38]→[14,26], [8,32]→[14,14], [14,14]→[20,14], [14,26]→[20,26] or [20,14], [20,14]↔[20,20]↔[20,26]. LEFT at [20,14]→[20,20] (moves right!).", "goal_model": "Navigate to blue target at bottom right", "recent_findings": ["LEFT at [20,14]→[20,20] (moves right!)"], "open_questions": ["Where does RIGHT from [20,14] go?"], "current_plan": "Try RIGHT from [20,14] to find new chamber", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["DOWN at [20,26]", "DOWN at [20,20] (twice)", "UP at [20,20]", "DOWN at [8,26]", "DOWN at [8,20]", "RIGHT at [20,26]", "DOWN at [8,14]"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
